era_context: Middle Bronze Age II. The Old Babylonian Empire was in its final decades of decline. The Hittite Old Kingdom was consolidating power in Anatolia. Egypt was fragmented in the Second Intermediate Period, with Asiatic settlement increasing in the Delta. The Indus Valley civilization was in its final urban phase or early post-urban transition. Mycenaean Greece was in the Early Bronze Age III. This was a period of significant state reorganization and population movement across the Near East.

events.title: Samsu-iluna's reign: Babylonian military campaigns and territorial loss
events.description: Samsu-iluna, son of Hammurabi, conducted military campaigns to defend Babylonian territory against encroaching regional powers. However, these campaigns were largely unsuccessful. The kingdom lost control of outlying regions and was increasingly confined to the Babylon heartland. Cuneiform texts record military setbacks and the growing power of rival kingdoms. This period marked the beginning of the end for the Old Babylonian Empire, which would fall to the Kassites around 1600 BCE.

sources.name: Babylonian king lists and administrative texts
Cuneiform letters and administrative documents from Babylon

events.certainty_note: Samsu-iluna's military difficulties are attested in cuneiform sources. His reign is dated c. 1749–1712 BCE, placing -1704 in his middle years. Exact dating varies by ±10–20 years.

events.title: Hittite expansion and consolidation in Anatolia
events.description: The Hittite Old Kingdom continued to expand its territorial control and consolidate its administrative and military structures. The kingdom was establishing itself as a major regional power, rivaling Babylon and Egypt. Hittite kings conducted campaigns in northern Syria and Mesopotamia, taking advantage of Babylonian weakness. The Hittites were also adopting and adapting Mesopotamian administrative practices and cuneiform writing.
key_figures: Hantili I or his successor (exact ruler uncertain)

sources.name: Later Hittite annals and king lists

events.certainty_note: Hittite expansion in this period is attested, but specific campaigns and their dating are poorly preserved.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact chronology of the Old Babylonian period is contested. Different chronological systems place Samsu-iluna's reign at different dates, affecting the interpretation of events in -1704. Some scholars emphasize external military pressure as the cause of Babylonian decline; others argue for internal administrative failure or climate stress. The precise causes and timeline of the decline remain debated.
historiographic_note: Documentation for -1704 is minimal and indirect. No contemporary records specifically document events of this year. The historical narrative is reconstructed from king lists, administrative texts, and later chronicles. The record is heavily skewed toward Mesopotamia and Anatolia; other regions are essentially invisible in written sources. Archaeological evidence provides some context but cannot pinpoint events to specific years. Chronological uncertainty of ±50 years is typical for this period. This year is largely a placeholder in the historical record, representing a broader pattern of Babylonian decline and Hittite expansion.

graph_edges.note: Babylonian military decline and Hittite expansion were concurrent, with the Hittites benefiting from Mesopotamian fragmentation.
Continuation of the broader decline process documented in -1703.
